tool.name: recetox-xMSannotator: Compute Isotopes
tool.description: detect and assign isotopic peaks to annotated compounds

configfiles.configfile:
## Load input tables annotation <- load_table("$annotation", "$annotation.ext") adduct_weights <- load_table("$adduct_weights", "$adduct_weights.ext") peak_table <- load_table("$peak_table", "$peak_table.ext")
## Compute isotope assignments annotation <- recetox.xmsannotator::compute_isotopes( annotation = annotation, adduct_weights = adduct_weights, intensity_deviation_tolerance = $intensity_deviation_tolerance, mass_defect_tolerance = $mass_defect_tolerance, peak_table = peak_table, rt_tolerance = $time_tolerance )
## Save output save_table(annotation, "$output_file", "$output_file.ext")

param.name: annotation
param.label: Annotation table
param.help: Annotation table from Simple Annotation tool (optionally with mass defect computed). Should contain compound annotations with chemical_ID, mz, rt columns.

param.name: adduct_weights
param.label: Adduct weights table
param.help: Table of adduct weights used for scoring. Contains information about expected adduct types and their relative abundances.

param.name: peak_table
param.label: Peak table with RT clusters and mass defect
param.help: Peak table containing: - peak: Peak identifier - mz: Mass-to-charge ratio - rt: Retention time - module: Module assignment from peak clustering - rt_cluster: RT cluster assignment - MD: Mass defect (if computed)

param.name: intensity_deviation_tolerance
param.label: Intensity deviation tolerance
param.help: Maximum allowed deviation from theoretical isotope intensity ratios. Default 0.1 means ±10% deviation is acceptable.

param.name: mass_defect_tolerance
param.label: Mass defect tolerance
param.help: Maximum difference in mass defect between a peak and its theoretical isotope. Helps filter false positive isotope assignments.

tool.help:
**Compute Isotopes**
Detect and assign isotopic peaks (M+1, M+2, etc.) to annotated compounds.
Description ===========
This tool identifies isotopic peaks that correspond to each annotated compound. Isotopic peaks arise from natural abundance heavy isotopes (¹³C, ²H, ¹⁵N, ¹⁸O, ³⁴S, etc.) and provide valuable confirmation of compound identity.
Isotope Detection Criteria --------------------------
A peak is assigned as an isotope of a compound when ALL of the following conditions are met:
1. **Mass Accuracy**: The peak's m/z matches the theoretical isotope mass within tolerance 2. **Retention Time**: The peak elutes within the specified RT window of the monoisotopic peak 3. **Intensity Ratio**: The intensity ratio matches theoretical isotope patterns within tolerance 4. **Mass Defect**: The mass defect is consistent with the expected isotope pattern 5. **Module Membership**: The isotope peak is in the same correlation module as the parent
Parameters ----------
**Intensity Deviation Tolerance** Maximum allowed deviation from theoretical isotope intensity ratios (default: 0.1 = ±10%).
For example, if the theoretical M+1 intensity is 10% of M, and you set tolerance to 0.1, then observed intensities between 9% and 11% would be accepted.
**Mass Defect Tolerance** Maximum allowed difference in mass defect between observed and theoretical isotope. Helps filter false positives from co-eluting compounds.
**Retention Time Tolerance** Maximum RT difference (in seconds) between the monoisotopic peak and its isotopes. Isotopes should co-elute with the parent compound.
Inputs ------
**Annotation Table** Output from Simple Annotation (optionally with mass defect computed).
**Adduct Weights Table** Contains information about expected adduct types for scoring.
**Peak Table with RT Clusters** Should include module assignments and RT cluster information from previous steps.
Outputs -------
**Annotation Table with Isotopes** The annotation table with additional columns: - `ISgroup`: Isotope group identifier - `isotope_rank`: Rank of this peak within its isotope group (1 = monoisotopic) - `n_isotopes`: Number of isotopes detected for this compound
Downstream Usage ----------------
Isotope information is used by: - **Reformat Annotation**: Prepares data for chemscore computation - **Compute Chemscore**: More isotopes = higher confidence score - **Compute Confidence Levels**: Isotope count affects confidence level assignment
References ----------
Silva, G. M., et al. (2018). Accurate prediction of isotopic patterns for metabolite identification in mass spectrometry. Bioinformatics, 34(17), i763-i771. DOI: 10.1093/bioinformatics/bty581
Kind, T., & Fiehn, O. (2007). Seven Golden Rules for heuristic filtering of molecular formulas obtained by accurate mass spectrometry. BMC Bioinformatics, 8, 105. DOI: 10.1186/1471-2105-8-105
